CURRENT AFFAIRS,

that the directors of the G. §. and W. Rail-
wa‘;'veaxi:oélemir meeting on Friday, decided, in answer to
the influential requisition to'which we drew attention
last week, upon calling an extraordinary general
meeting. ~ They could have done nothing _else.
One of their directors, Frederick C. Pilkington, D.L.,
J.P., is also a director of the ill-fated National Assur-
ance,Compa.ny, the collapse of which is a national cala-
mity, and will probably bring ruin into many a home.
All the directors of the National Assurance Company
were possessors of highly respectable names; the direc-
tors of the G. S. and W. Railway are, qlso, al_l highly
respectable persons, one & D.L. and J. .P. is s0 lnglgy I‘;e-
spectable that he is also a director of the }ll-fa.tet a-
" tional Assurance Company. TUnder th.e cxrcurfnsda.nces
the directors of the G. S. and W. Railway, of depre-

ciated dividends, acted wisely in deciding at once, at the
request of influential sharcholders, to call the extraor-
dinary general meeting. The capital value of the Or-
dinary Shares in the market have fallen nearly to the
extent of three million pounds sterling since such a re-
cent date as 1898.  That fact is enough to arrest the
attention of the most confiding and apathetic share-
holder. Many people who have money, but no know-
ledge of finance and no opinion of their own upon pub-
lic - companies, trust blindly in names—in highly re-
spectable names. The names of the directors of the
G. S. and W. Railway are all of the highest respecta-
bility ; but so are the names of the directors of the Na-
tional Assurance Company, and Frederick C. Pilking-
ton, D.L., J.P., is a director of both. No doubt, the
directors of the G. S. and W. Railway—keen, commer-
cial men, if not able railway directors, as we are sure
they all are—saw at once that the terrible collapse of
the National Assurance Company had considerably dis-
counted highly respectable names in the Irish investors’
world, and that a refusal to summon the extraordinary
general meeting might have turned a healthy spirit of
inquiry and mild disfrust in directors’ infallibility into
a very ugly and aggravated situafion, if not a panic.
We congratulate them on their promptitude in deciding
to call the meeting ; in doing so, they acted in the best
interests of themselves, their shareholders, and public
credit. We have no doubt that nothing but good will
come out of the Committee of Enquiry, which we trust
that the extraordinary general meeting will agree to
appoint. Shareholders should now lose no delay in for-
warding their proxies to Mr. M. A. Sullivan, LL.B.,
Belclere, Temple Gardens, Dublin. This is a purely
business matter; and it is a very important business
matter in the commercial economy of this country.

Father T. A. Finlay gave out some general idea re-
cently about a voluntary University with possibly a
corrugated iron roof. In the current number of the
New Ireland Review he does justice to himself and to
his idea by dealing with the matter at length in a most
interesting article which we commend to the careful con-
sideration of our readers. His article is entitled “ A
Policy on the University Question;’’ and the aim of
Father Finlay is to find an ad snterim policy making
for a real solution. Father Finlay sketches briefly the dif-
ferent conditions that exist to-day compared with those
that existed in the time of the attempt which was made
to establish a University fifty years ago. Degrees may
now be had from the Royal University, and the Inter-
mediatg system has trained large numbers in the course
leading to University studies. Father Finlay proposes
the establishment of a National University College.
He proposes that money should be raised by a subsecrip-
tion of £10 from each of the 1,087 ecclesiastical parishes
of the country, the money raised in each diocese should
be devoted to scholarships for the ablest intermediate
school pupils in the diocese. If the value of a scholar-
ship were fixed at £50, then £10,000 would provide
free education for 200 students. If £40 went for main-
tenance and £10 for school fees, a sum of £2,000 would
go to the latter. Would this be sufficient for the main-
tenance of an adequate teaching staff? Father Finlay
assumes that the services of half the Fellows of the
Royal University would, as at present, be given to' the
Dublin College, and that this addition of teaching
power would supply the deficiency. As for buildin,
and equipment, if the Catholic Hierarchy threw their
weight in with the scheme, it is assumed that the build-

ing in Stephen’s Green, of which they are trustees, would et
be available. Enlargement would be necessary and

the enlargement could be made of the cheapest materials
—wood and iron. This proposed wood and iron exten-

e g

178

" sion of premises is one of the strong points of Father
- Finlay’s scheme. But then these are fo stand for much

_ more than wood and iron. A corrugated iron lecture
* hall standing beside Lord Iveagh’s town residence is to
preach its own eloquent sermon so long as it is permitted
to stand there—preach it to natives and visitors—visitors
royal or otherwise.

The plan set forth involves only a public contribution
of about £10,000 a year, each diocese to spend the
amount it raises on granting scholarships to students
within its own boundaries. The scheme is not put for-
ward as a substitute for Government aid; the duty of
\ the Government is admitted, but the Government re-
fuses to do its duty; this scheme will stimulate the
Government to do its duty. An University in heing
once created, the question of endowment will call for a
mere yes or no from the British Parliament.

Father Finlay’s idea as set out in some detail in his
article in' the New Ireland Review, is worthy of the
attention of the thoughful people of the country. If
once the £10,000 a year were collected, there appears
to us to be no serious difficulties to the carrying out
of his idea. Of course £10,000 is only a bagatelle even
‘to such a poor country as Ireland; the country would
not miss it if it lost it, but it is rather difficult to collect,
such a sum all the same. As a means to precipitate a
permanent solution of the University difficulty we think
that, the scheme would prove a potent force.

== It is pleasant to read, at shareholders’ meetings of

Trvish Industrial Companies, a tale of prosperity. Kapp

and Peterson, Ltd., the well-known Irish pipe manufac-

turers, ete., are thriving on Irish industry. The Chair-

man at the meeting had a pleasant tale to tell of in-

creased dividends and continued prosperity. In the

|4 course of his remarks, he said :—* During the half-year,

: in face of the general depression in trade, they had

found both their home and export trade showing im-

provement, and they were sanguine of very satisfactory

results likely to accrue from the pushing of their manu-

factures both-in America and Canada. The Company

continued in every way to prosper, and the

board had no hesitation in saying that the outlook at

the present moment was brighter than ever. He

thought that was an opportune moment to call attention

_ to the fact that the industry was purely Irish, and had

grown from a small beginning to be a very powerful

industry indeed, employing a great many Dublin
people.”’

Dennehy’s hats must be disturbing the market—a

~ good sign. There appears to be a particular set made
upon the firm. Recently a Dublin correspondent wrote

to the Cork Industrial Development Association stating
that a Dublin hatter, whom he had asked for one of
Dennehy’s hats, informed him that he had ceased stock-
ing them as he had found that they only made portion

of them and imported the remainder. We take the
following from the report in the Cork Sun:—The

- Secretary of the Cork Industrial Association stated
that on receipt of letter he paid a surprise visit to
Messrs. Dennehy’s factory in Hanover Street, and there
he saw hats being made in every stage, from the blowing
of the raw fur on to a cone-shaping machine to the com-
pletion of the work, leaving the hat ready for use by a
customer. He was, therefore, in a position to state
Messrs. Dennehy and Sons make every part of a hat.
They employ over 80 hands at that work, and the in-
spection of their factory and the witnessing of that
" number of employees busy at their various occupations
in converting the raw fur into hats was most interest-
- ing. The proprietors stated their business was rapidly
increasing, not alone owing to the greater increase of
late in the demand of Irish-made goods, but also to the
 fact that they were in a position to compete with any
of their foreign competitors, except, perhaps, for very
poor quality hats.  The Secretary said that no one
[ isit that factory and see the busy hive of -in-
it represented, without feeling that it de-

served every encouragement.’””  That ought to settle
the matter for some time. We have no doubt that in
a few months time some other tale will be set afloat by
some one or other who would sooner that Irish-made
hats did not increase their hold on the Irish market.

Many a fine, thumping, true blue bigot up from the
country tramped the Dublin pavements last week. Unc-
tuous rectitude has been holding forth on many a
platform, and oratory, combining a choice mixture of
bigotry, rant and hypocrisy has been on tap in many
places. One Lord Langford, K.C.V.0O. (whatever
those letters stand for), sat in the chair at an “Irish
Church Mission ”’ in Townsend Street, on May 2nd.
This ““ nobleman "’—we wonder what does he do for
his living and by way of justification for his existence—
said, according to the report in the Irish Times:—
“He looked upon it as a privilege to be there that
afternoon. He always derived.a blessing when he came
and stood upon a platform where God’s help was being
asked. He knew that great blessings would come over
that mission in the land. When they met at a meeting
of that description it was for only one great purpose,
that of their dear and bountiful Master, who gave them
their faith. It was a great joy to him to come there
that afternoon and to recognise that there was a great
spirit of revival in that dear old City of Dublin.
(Applause).”  This is the “ mission’” in connection
with which the notorious Dr. Long, of Limerick, plies °
his calling greatly to the detriment of peace and good
will in that City. A Rev. P. B. Johnson at the same
meeting, said:—“They were not half sympathetic
enough with their brothers and sisters in the remote
parts of Ireland. (Hear, hear.) He had the privilege
of spending some little time with Dr. Long, whose dis-
pensaries were now very well attended. Dr. Long had
won a complete victory over all the opposition that had
troubled him so long, and the people were flocking into
his dispensary. (Applause.) As many as 56 patients
were there in one morning, and most of these were
Roman Catholics. That was something to be thankful
for, when they considered the opposition so great by
priests and the mob. Dr. Long was becoming quite
popular in Limerick. That ought to give them great
cause for encouragemnt.’’

The Aghadoe affair cropped up at the General Synod
of the Church of England’s Faihtful Garrison in Ire-
and.  One Major MacGillicuddy, brought up the
matter before the Synod. The son of Axrchdeacon
Orpen was selected by the Board of Patronage against
the wishes of the parishioners, for the comfortable
“living *’ in the parish of Aghadoe, in the “Idolatrous’”
kingdom of Kerry. There cannot be much work to be
done in this “living ’ for the “Saved’’ congregation
is very small. Major MacGillicuddy pointed out ‘that
the parishioners were the descendants of Huguenots and
Cromwellian soldiers. The clergyman foisted upon the
arishioners was now, according to Major MacGilli-
cuddy, practically boycotted. What! we thought 1t
was only base “Idolatrous” United Irish Leaguers
and people of that kind who boycotted people! What
are we coming to when we find a “Saved ”’ clergyman
boycotted by his “ Saved congregation. According
to the “Saved ”’ Bishop of Limerick, “ the church was
nailed up one Sunday, and on another Sunday red
pepper had been set on fire in the church.”” Did ever
one hear of such lawlessness. What was a perpetual
Coercion Act passed for if not to put down such law-
breakers as the Red Pepper Boys. Picture a band of
dour descendants of Cromwellian soldiers stealing
stealthily on the Aghadoe “Saved ”’ church, the leader
armed with a hammer and some tenpenny nails, and
the followers each “with a bible in one hand and
a ha’penny-worth of red pepper in the other. We see
them filing along the bye-ways leading to the church,

we almost hear the Red Pepper Boys with their solemn 3
Cromwellian voices singing hymns. We almost hear

a refrain “Put your trust in nails, me hoys, and keep

your pepper dry.”” By Old Noll, no clergyman will be ‘

foisted on these doughty descendants of Cromwellian

Aghadoe to say to that?

. One day last week the annual meeting of the
Seriptite Readers and Ivish Society was held, and a let-
ter was read from the Protestant Archbishop of Dublin
acéepting the post of patron to the United Scripture
Readers and Irish Society. In the course of the report
read by Rév. Mr. J. C. Irwin, we read :— In the Irish-
speaking districts the old plan of using local teachers to
instruct their neighbours is still followed. Since 1818
this method has been used, and by it many thousands
have been reached and inflienced who could not be
veached in any other way. There are large numbers
still dependent on the Irish language for instruction, for
they cannot speak and think freely in English. With
the Irish Language Movement, so far as it aims to re-
establish Irish as the language of every-day. life in Ire-
land where English is now spokel, this socicty is not in
any way identified. If is a Bible:téaching society, and
uses language only as a means to that end. From the
report of the society established for promoting this lan-
guage revival, it appears that it has made enormous pro-
gréss. For instance, in schools where, in 1901, 4,092
pupils passed for spécial fees, last year (1904) the num-
ber was 25,984!  Of the publications of the society
326,000 have been sold. All this gives opportunity for
the dissemination of the Holy Scriptures in Irish.”

There ought to be a good time coming for the Terry
Geraghty breed who can lisp in Gaelic. Since the Pro-
testant Chapel on the South Circular Road held a much-
boomed Gaelic service on St. Patrick’s Day under the
disinfecting folds of the Union Jack, perhaps some
patriot of the United Scripture Readers and Irish So:
ciety would step forward and champion the Irish lan-
guage. He might succeed in getting himself co-opted
on the Executive of the Gaelic League.

At the meeting of this society, of which the Protes:
tant Archbishop is patron, the Rev. Thomas Boland
said : = There wés a crying need for rallying round thé
society, and taking a deep interest in their native coun-
try. They had no bad feeling towards their fellow-men.
They had heard & good deal about the ‘ Catholic Asso-
ciation,” but these in the city had not felt it, as they
were living in the lap of luxury. Its influence was felt
in the West of Ireland, where Protestants were few in
number and isolated.”” Certainly that Catholic Associa-
tion drove terror into their hearts. Wade Thompson
should rush to the rescue of the persecuted “saved ’’ of
the Wiest—those persecuted people who thrive in West-

creating bitterness and bad blood.

One night last week, Father T. A. Finlay read a
paper before the Statistical and Social Enquiry Society,
on Practical Education in our Schools.”  In the
course of his paper, as reported, he said:—“We fre-
quently meet with advertisements of schools in which
the successes of their pupils in the competitive examina-
tions for bank clerkships, the railway services, the Post
ffice; the Excise or other departments of the Civil

vice are set forth with much self-complacency, Have
e ever seen an advertisement in which an Irish school
aimed the merit of having produced a Successful
wrmer, of having taught a boy how to cultivate a
r trained a girl in the arts of housewifery? In
espects our schools offer a striking contrast to the

THE LEADER.

port, for instance, where the Scotchman recently went

kindred institutions in such countries as Belgium,
France; or Gevmany.’ It is not “class’ to be a
farmer in this country. Belgium, France and Ger-
many are mere clod-hopping countries compared with
I-ah:land. Why, one of our bank clerks is centuries
in advance of the mere German manufacturer.

We make another quotation from Father Finlay’s
address:—“ Our school system should provide for the
amelioration of the life our people have actually to lead;
and of the industry in which they are actually engaged,
rather than aim at introducing new industries and
new consequent conditions of life. -This latter task is
beyond the power of the schoolmaster. It seems to me
a fruitless and impolitic effort to teach children the
methods of an industry which they will not have any
opportuniby of practising in their own country. Educa-
tion of this kind is only an equipment for emigration,

-and We have no call to expend our resources in educat-

ing citizens for other nations.” ~What has the De-
partment with all its funds and its regiment of salaried
officials done for existing industries? What has it
done in the practical way of endeavouring to obtain
Ttish purchasers for Irish goods? One great sure prac-
tical way to inc¢rease Irish industry is to increase the
demand for the class of goods that Ireland is actually
produ¢ing.  What did the Department do in that
direction? Why it has not, with all its money and its
officials placed as much as a detective on the road with
a view to protecting the manufacturers and the public
from fraud in the way of traders putting foreign goods
on the market as Irish. ~But these are low-down prac-
tical matters; and the Vice-President was engaged in
writing a “ philosophic *’ book !

In the course of a letter, Councillor Robert
W. Farrell, of Lewisham, London, says:—The
LeapEr has given me encouragement, and I often
wish I was in Ireland to lend a practical hand in thg
Trish-Ireland movement. I do my best by sending to
Ireland for as many things as I can without paying
more than I should heré, and your advertisers I find do
actually—in the several instances I have put it to the
test—supply rather better goods, price for price, than
I get here.””

In the course of a private letter from a well-known
man in a Southern town, our correspondent says:==
“By the way, I heard to-day from that a big
Dublin publican told a large spirit merchant here that
‘ the trade’ had not got such a blow since the days of
Father Mathew as the Gaelic League and the paper
called: the LEeader had given it.”” Well, more of that
to “ the trade;’’ as far as most of the publicans are con=
‘cerned, what they lose by the drop in the sale of drink
they ought to be able to gain by a rise in the sale of
groceries, bread, butter, and other things.

An TIrish priest in America informs us that there is
a lady, who for some years past, has gone every Spring
to Ireland from Winnipeg, Man., Canada, with money
to pay the passages of servant girls. He says:— “ Now
I beg of you to warn Catholic girls against taking this
free passage, because on their arrival here, they are
obliged to go wherever their passage money came from,
into the country where there are no chapels, or to
places not at all suitable for them. They practically
sell themselves until they pay back every cent. of their
fare.”’

We have received a cutting’ with sketch of a hoe

‘manufactured by Messrs. John McKay and Son, St.

John’s Iron Works, Cork. In the letterpress accom-
panying it we are pleased to see that the firm claim that.
this Irish-made implement, which is sold at £2 2s. 0d.
surpasses all imported hoes. We are informed that
Messrs. MoKay’s hoe is made of steel and malleable
combining lightness with strength, and can be
and readily changed to suit every variety of

. Bulgaden is a rural parish a few miles from Kilmal-

lock in the County of Limerick. Irish has been taught
to the school children for the past six years. Recently
Irish classes for grown people and Irish dancing classes
have been carried on. Bulgaden’s first Irish concert
was held last Sunday evening. The programme is be-
fore us, and it comprises no less than thirty items, and
i3 made up of Irish and Anglo-Irish songs, music and
exhibitions of dancing.  The programme would do
honour to any large provincial town, and a rural parish
like Bulgaden has every reason to be congratulated
upon its first Irish concert.

Recently there was a Sgopurveact at the Ursuline
Convent, Blackrock, Cork. It was a more or less pri-
vate affair. There is a domestic economy class in con-
nection with fhis convent school. It is, as its name im-
plies, for the practical purpose of training pupils in
dressmaking, cookery, and generally fitting them for
household work and fulfiling woman’s duties. There
was practical proof given of some of the efficiency of
the work done in those departments. The pupils them-
selves not only made all the good things set before their
guests during the interval of the entertainment, but
also served them around and looked after all the wants
of those present for the evening. The pupils, we hear,
were also responsible for the staging of the plays, and
the making of the dresses and costumes worn.
Amongst the songs was Caicilin 11 hutlacamn  and
a special feature of the entertainmer.t was a character
dence in which Ireland and the four provinces were
depicted with characteristic dress, badges, and shields.
Perhaps the next Sgopuiveacc given at this convent
will not be kept so private; the nuns may see their way
to invite the fathers and brothers of the pupils as well
as their lady friends. We are glad to hear that Irish
is taught five days in the week at this Ursuline Convent.

The harbingers of the coming of summer in Ireland
are the swallow and the Aeridheacht. An Aeridreacht,
under the auspices of the Father O’Growney branch of
the Gaelic League will be held next Sunday, commencing
at 4 o’clock, at Drumcondra.

‘We have received an Irish version of a lecture de-
livered many years ago by William O’Brien, on the
Irish language. The lecture was originally written in
English. It has now been turned into Irish by Ta 03
O Mupcéada and published by the Gaelic League,
Upper O’Connell Street, Dublin, at 6d. nett.

Our contemporary, the Leinster Leader, devoted its
leading article in its last issue to a severe criticism of

the Gaelic League. We are not aware that they have
any procession in connection with the League in Naas,

so the chances of the Editor of our contemporary being
treated, by way of reply, to criminal street blackguar-
dism in the name of the League by unauthorised per-
sons is remote.

The remarks of our Kildare contemporary are very
severe. “ A lot of cheap and uncritical palaver has
been lavished on some of these organisers and instruc-
tors; it is about time it ceased,”’ says our contem-
porary in the course of its article. The recent refusal
of a majority of the Executive at the last meeting of
the League to condemn criminal street blackguardism
committed in the name of the League, has somewhat
arrested the flow of  cheap and uncritical palaver,” and
has instead turned critical eyes on the- Organisation.
The League as a League is all
though it needs overhauling, alterations and repairs,
and the spread of the Irish Language is necessary for
the creation of a distinct Irish civilization.

‘A request for subscriptions for the Gaelic League
Organisation Fund was sent to each priest—we wonder
are they sending such requests to all the parsons—at a
certain institution in the country. The reply of the

right, .

May 13, 1905.

priests, five in number, was a joint note that they would
give nothing this year nor in fu}mre years until there was
a proper apology for the criminal street blackguardism
committed in the name the League at the Laug}mge
Procession in Dublin. Our informant was not in a
position to give the name of Lh(.: 1_nsh1tut19n for pub-
licity unless he had received Rermlsslon—whxph he could
not  possibly do in  time for  this issue—
and the text of the reply, if he is
permitted  to  give it pu})liciby, cannot fo}v
like reason appear in our present issue. Do the aubhor%»
ties of the Gaclic League want to break up the organi-
sation? Surely they ought to know that the self-re-
specting and decent people of this country will not con-
tinue to repose confidence in an organisation !;he
majority of the Executive of which at their last meeting
deliberately and ostentatiously refused to denounce
criminal street blackguardism. Might we ask the indi-
viduals who compose the majority of the Executive at
the last meeting, and other individuals a_lsq, to examine
their consciences? An organisation intimately con-
nected with< movement for the moral and national up-
lifting of the Irish nation is a dangerous machine to
tamper with in hot or bad blood, and it is an awful
thing to contemplate that spite, ignoble passion, in-
flamed  prejudice, or  pettiness shou}d glut
themselves regardless of the effect on an important
organisation.

Since writing the above, and as we prepare for Press,
we have received a copy of the letter sent by the priests
referred to in reply to the circular letter from the head-
quarters of the League. The priests, however, do not
wish their names, or the name of the establishment to
which they are attached to published :—The Se-
cretary, Gaelic League :—Sir—In reply to your circular
re funds for the Gaelic League, I beg to state that, un-
like former years, I cannot see my way to contribute to
a League that will not condemn a street outrage carried
out under the direction of an individual wearing its offi-
cial badge. No equivocation can, in the least, take the
blame from off your shoulders. And you should at once,
as became gentlemen, have tendered an apology to the
gentlemen insulted, one of whom, at least, was your in-
vited guest. Whatever enthusiasm I and those around
me have for the Gaelic League and its ideal, has
been imbibed solely from the pages of the LEADER; to it
alone is due the existence of the League outside Dublin

in the ex-lip~rebel " confrere "

THE MANUFACTURERS AND THE INDUSTRIAL
REVIYAL.

E publish this week yet another interesting
batch of letters from Irish manufacturers.

The firm of Winstanley, who do a great and popular
boot trade, wholesale and retail, are very emphatic.
They have received benefit from the Industrial Revival
Movement, and they write:—“If the result of the
Irish Industrial Revival Movement is to create in the
minds of purchasers the impression that in buying Irish-
made goods they are paying extra price simply because
the goods are Irish, I would candidly prefer that the
movement never existed.””  Instead of claiming any
grant-in-aid from their customers, Messrs. Shuley and
Co. say :—“ It is marvellous the number of people who,
if the Irish-made article is even better and cheaper than
the imported, will still give the preference to the
foreigner for paper, stationery and colour printing.’’ This 55
habit of giving not only a preference, but even more
than a preference, to foreign goods, is one that we, from
the start, have done our best to kill ; and we have hit it !
badly, if we have not yet killed it; but the uncon-
sidered cry of giving more for Irish goods because they &
are Irish—a cry that has met with unanimous condem- j
nation from the manufacturers, who have, so far, re- =
plied to our letter—that cry is calculated to hamper the ‘
efforts of a sound and business-like movement, and in-
crease that class of people to whom Messrs. John
Shuley and Co. refer, who pass over Irish work even
when better and cheaper than foreign work. The firm
of Barmak, Ltd., also refer to the prejudice against ]
Irish manufacturers that still exists. Their statement b
that their English trade is about twenty times as great i
as their trade in Ireland is instructive. All Barmak,
Ltd., want is that Irishmen should give up the idea
that because goods are made in Ireland they must neces-
sarily be inferior to the foreign goods. Unfortunately
that view even still exists, and the talk of those who
orate about the duty of giving more for goods because
they are made in the country, unfortunately goes to pre-
serve and strengthen that view, when the great need of
the manufacturers and those depending upon them for ’
employment is, that the prejudice against Irish manu-
facture should be cleared away. Those of our readers i

who are likely to be engaged in arguments on this ques-
tion should preserve the replies of the various manufac-
turers. They may come in handy. Even an ounce of
evidence is better than a monster bubble of raimeis.

Messrs. I. S. Varian and Co., North Dublin Brush
Factory, 91 and 92 Talbot Street, write:—

“Dear Sir—In your letter of April 15th, you wish
our opinion upon the effect of two classes of support to
Irvish manufacturers—viz., whether it is for the general
good of Irish manufacturers to give them a sentimental
support regardless of the value offered, and of the pecu-
niary sacrifice (if necessary) on the part of the pur-
chaser; or only where the articles offered are of equal
value to any other procurable.

“We consider that sentimental support may be ser-
viceable to a budding industry, but if applied to the -
general manufacturers of the country must have a fatal
effect, as fthey will then live, develop, or die as the
public whim of the momenf affects them. With a wave
of feeling favouring native industries bogus manufac-
tures will spring into a short-lived existence tor die
again with its subsidence. It, therefore, seems to us
clear that the only durable basis is that of value equal
to like goods in the world’s markets. National senti-
ment has done, and can do, much for our manufactures
by giving an easy entrance to them, by giving all Irish
quotations careful attention, and where small irregulari-
ties exist, giving advice and aid to meet the competition
to which they are opposed.

“This sentiment also gives the Irish consumer a
patience most commendable in many cases, enabling the
manufacturer of some new thing to meet his competition
and correct mistakes; but beyond this no customer can
be expected to go for any length of time.

“ Any manufacturer selling his goods above market
values cannot have a growing trade, and what trade he
does is subject to some accident of position or sentiment.

“ Fifty years before this  Revival ’ we sold our goods
simply because they were the best value in the market ;
since then this system has absorbed the major portion
of the Irish trade into our hands, and makes it now as
easy for us to do the Scotch trade, which we are opening
up as our home one; if our trade existed on public
generosity, it would be quite useless for us to look for it
where that did not exist.

“The trade of any country. existing on its National
sentiments must be confined to itself, and then only par-
tially ; therefore its trade must be curtailed indefinitely.
—Respectfully,

“I. 8. VaraN & Co.”

Irish manufactured goods, we beg to say that we, as
manufacturers, do not desire a grant-in-aid from our
customers or the public. We entered the market with
coods made in Ireland ; but not on this score do we look
?or support, but because we produce feeding meals,
cakes, etc., which we hold to be able to meet keen,
foreign competition in price and quality, and beat

their college course. Some fifty years ago the Irish Col-
lege of Surgeons was an easy first in the English-speak-
ing world. ~These were the days of Corrigan and Graves,
of Stokes and Carmichael, of Colles and of Porter.
Now it is a poor third straggling long below London,
and even behind Edinburgh. Some of the LEADER
readers are averse to Irishmen taking their share in the
posts which are to be had in the British Empire. This,
I believe, is a mistake. ~We pay the taxes, and we i
should have our share, like our Scotch friends, in the
loaves and fishes. In almost every district in England
during the last fifty years the Irish doctor has helped
the National Cause equally with the Irish priest; now
he is rapidly passing away to be replaced by an English
or a Scotchman.

The late Professor Tichborne was one of those who re-
presented Ireland in the General Medical Council. Now °
that he is, unhappily, passed away, a successor must be
appointed. The election will take place on Friday next,
and it behoves us to see that a Catholic and a Nationalist,
shall be appointed.

Colonel Adye Curran, M.D., F.R.C.S., has offered
himself as a candidate, and I think that he should get =
the support of admirers of the Leaper policy. He is
not exactly a hill-sider or a tin-piker, but he has been boy-
cotted by the bigots of Rathmines for being a Papist.

Even the fact that he ranks as a Colonel in the British
army could not wipe out this stain in the eyes of Rath-
mines, and he was declared unfit to be even on the Board
of a Fever Hospital. But because of the bigotry of
Rathmines he is likely to be elected to the General Medi-
cal Council on Friday next—the most important post
for a medical man in the three Kingdoms.
